text_blocks.text:
• 
Software functionality that exercises control authority over potentially safety-significant 
hardware systems, subsystems, or components, allowing time for predetermined safe 
detection and intervention by independent safety mechanisms to mitigate or control the 
mishap or hazard. 
(This definition includes the control of moderately complex system/software functionality, no 
parallel processing, or few interfaces, but other safety systems/mechanisms can partially 
mitigate. System and software fault detection and annunciation notifies the control entity of 
the need for required safety actions.)
text_blocks.normalized_text:
•
Software functionality that exercises control authority over potentially safety-significant
hardware systems, subsystems, or components, allowing time for predetermined safe
detection and intervention by independent safety mechanisms to mitigate or control the
mishap or hazard.
(This definition includes the control of moderately complex system/software functionality, no
parallel processing, or few interfaces, but other safety systems/mechanisms can partially
mitigate. System and software fault detection and annunciation notifies the control entity of
the need for required safety actions.)

text_blocks.text:
• 
Software item that displays safety-significant information requiring immediate operator entity 
to execute a predetermined action for mitigation or control over a mishap or hazard. 
Software exception, failure, fault, or delay will allow, or fail to prevent, mishap occurrence. 
(This definition assumes that the safety-critical display information may be time-critical, but 
the time available does not exceed the time required for adequate control entity response 
and hazard control.)
text_blocks.normalized_text:

the time available does not exceed the time required for adequate control entity response
and hazard control.)

blocks.text:
4.4.2 Software Safety Criticality Matrix. The SSCM (Table V) uses Table I severity 
categories for the columns and Table IV software control categories for the rows. Table V 
assigns SwCI numbers to each cross-referenced block of the matrix. The SSCM shall define the 
LOR tasks associated with the specific SwCI. Although it is similar in appearance to the Risk 
Assessment Matrix (Table III), the SSCM is not an assessment of risk. The LOR tasks 
associated with each SwCI are the minimum set of tasks required to assess the software 
contributions to the system-level risk.

blocks.text:
4.4.3 Assessment of software contribution to risk. All software contributions to system 
risk, including any results of Table VI application, shall be documented in the HTS.
blocks.normalized_text:
4.4.3 Assessment of software contribution to risk. All software contributions to system
risk, including any results of Table VI application, shall be documented in the HTS.

blocks.text:
a. The Table V LOR tasks shall be performed to assess the software contributions to the 
system-level risk. Results of the LOR tasks provide a level of confidence in safety-significant 
software and document causal factors and hazards that may require mitigation. Results of the 
LOR tasks shall be included in the risk management process. Appendix B provides an example 
of how to assign a risk level to software contributions to system risk identified by completing the 
LOR analysis.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. The Table V LOR tasks shall be performed to assess the software contributions to the
system-level risk. Results of the LOR tasks provide a level of confidence in safety-significant
software and document causal factors and hazards that may require mitigation. Results of the
LOR tasks shall be included in the risk management process. Appendix B provides an example
of how to assign a risk level to software contributions to system risk identified by completing the
LOR analysis.

blocks.text:
b. If the required LOR tasks are not performed, then the system risk(s) contributions 
associated with unspecified or incomplete LOR tasks shall be documented according to Table 
VI. Table VI depicts the relationship between SwCI, risk levels, completion of LOR tasks, and 
risk assessment.
blocks.normalized_text:
b. If the required LOR tasks are not performed, then the system risk(s) contributions
associated with unspecified or incomplete LOR tasks shall be documented according to Table
VI. Table VI depicts the relationship between SwCI, risk levels, completion of LOR tasks, and
risk assessment.

blocks.text:
c. All software contributions to system risk, including any results of Table VI 
application, shall be documented in the HTS. Perform risk acceptance in accordance with 
applicable DoDI 5000 series.
blocks.normalized_text:
c. All software contributions to system risk, including any results of Table VI
application, shall be documented in the HTS. Perform risk acceptance in accordance with
applicable DoDI 5000 series.

blocks.text:
5.1 Additional information. Individual tasks, Appendix A, and Appendix B contain 
optional information for developing program-specific requirements.
blocks.normalized_text:
5.1 Additional information. Individual tasks, Appendix A, and Appendix B contain
optional information for developing program-specific requirements.

blocks.text:
5.2 Tasks. The tasks in this Standard can be selectively applied to fit a tailored system 
safety effort. The 100-series tasks apply to management. The 200-series tasks apply to analysis. 
The 300-series tasks apply to evaluation. The 400-series tasks apply to verification. Each 
desired task shall be specifically called out in a contract because the task descriptions do not 
include requirements for any other tasks.
blocks.normalized_text:
5.2 Tasks. The tasks in this Standard can be selectively applied to fit a tailored system
safety effort. The 100-series tasks apply to management. The 200-series tasks apply to analysis.
The 300-series tasks apply to evaluation. The 400-series tasks apply to verification. Each
desired task shall be specifically called out in a contract because the task descriptions do not
include requirements for any other tasks.

blocks.text:
5.3 Task structure. Each individual task is divided into three parts—purpose, task 
description, and details to be specified.
blocks.normalized_text:
5.3 Task structure. Each individual task is divided into three parts—purpose, task
description, and details to be specified.

blocks.text: a. The purpose explains the rationale for performing the task.
blocks.normalized_text: a. The purpose explains the rationale for performing the task.

blocks.text:
b. The task description describes the work a contractor shall perform if the task is placed 
on contract. When preparing proposals, the contractor may recommend inclusion of additional 
tasks or deletion of specified tasks with supporting rationale for each addition/deletion.
blocks.normalized_text:
b. The task description describes the work a contractor shall perform if the task is placed
on contract. When preparing proposals, the contractor may recommend inclusion of additional
tasks or deletion of specified tasks with supporting rationale for each addition/deletion.

blocks.text:
c. The details to be specified in each task description lists specific information, additions, 
modifications, deletions, or options to the requirements of the task that should be considered 
when requiring a task. This information is then included in the contractual document along with 
the task number. The list provided with each task is not necessarily complete and may be 
supplemented. Any task selected should be specifically imposed by task number in the Request 
for Proposal (RFP) and Statement of Work (SOW). The details to be specified that are annotated 
with an “(R)” are required. The Government provides these details to the contractor for proper 
implementation of the task.
blocks.normalized_text:

when requiring a task. This information is then included in the contractual document along with

mitigation of hazards. It should be used not only by system safety professionals, but also by 
other functional disciplines such as fire protection engineers, occupational health professionals, 
and environmental engineers.

blocks.text:
TASK 101 
HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND MITIGATION EFFORT USING THE 
SYSTEM SAFETY METHODOLOGY
blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 101
HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND MITIGATION EFFORT USING THE
SYSTEM SAFETY METHODOLOGY

blocks.text:
TASK 101 
HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND MITIGATION EFFORT USING THE 
SYSTEM SAFETY METHODOLOGY
blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 101
HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND MITIGATION EFFORT USING THE
SYSTEM SAFETY METHODOLOGY

blocks.text: TASK 101
blocks.normalized_text: TASK 101

blocks.text:
101.1 Purpose. Task 101 is to integrate hazard identification and mitigation into the Department 
of Defense (DoD) acquisition Systems Engineering (SE) process using the system safety 
methodology. The goal should always be to eliminate the hazard if possible. When a hazard 
cannot be eliminated, the associated risk should be reduced to the lowest acceptable level within 
the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system safety design order of 
precedence.
blocks.normalized_text:
101.1 Purpose. Task 101 is to integrate hazard identification and mitigation into the Department
of Defense (DoD) acquisition Systems Engineering (SE) process using the system safety
methodology. The goal should always be to eliminate the hazard if possible. When a hazard
cannot be eliminated, the associated risk should be reduced to the lowest acceptable level within
the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system safety design order of
precedence.

text_blocks.text:
TASK 101 
HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND MITIGATION EFFORT USING THE 
SYSTEM SAFETY METHODOLOGY
text_blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 101
HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND MITIGATION EFFORT USING THE
SYSTEM SAFETY METHODOLOGY

text_blocks.text:
101.1 Purpose. Task 101 is to integrate hazard identification and mitigation into the Department 
of Defense (DoD) acquisition Systems Engineering (SE) process using the system safety 
methodology. The goal should always be to eliminate the hazard if possible. When a hazard 
cannot be eliminated, the associated risk should be reduced to the lowest acceptable level within 
the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system safety design order of 
precedence.

the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system safety design order of
precedence.

TASK 102
SYSTEM SAFETY PROGRAM PLAN

blocks.text: TASK 102
blocks.normalized_text: TASK 102

blocks.text:
102.1 Purpose. Task 102 is to develop a System Safety Program Plan (SSPP) that documents 
the system safety methodology for the identification, classification, and mitigation of safety 
hazards as part of the overall Systems Engineering (SE) process. The SSPP should be an integral 
part of the Systems Engineering Management Plan (SEMP). The SSPP shall detail the tasks and 
activities that are required to implement a systematic approach of hazard analysis, risk 
assessment, and risk management. The goal should always be to eliminate the hazard if possible. 
When a hazard cannot be eliminated, the associated risk should be reduced to the lowest 
acceptable level within the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system 
safety design order of precedence.
blocks.normalized_text:
102.1 Purpose. Task 102 is to develop a System Safety Program Plan (SSPP) that documents
the system safety methodology for the identification, classification, and mitigation of safety
hazards as part of the overall Systems Engineering (SE) process. The SSPP should be an integral
part of the Systems Engineering Management Plan (SEMP). The SSPP shall detail the tasks and
activities that are required to implement a systematic approach of hazard analysis, risk
assessment, and risk management. The goal should always be to eliminate the hazard if possible.
When a hazard cannot be eliminated, the associated risk should be reduced to the lowest
acceptable level within the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system
safety design order of precedence.

blocks.text:
102.2 Task description. The contractor shall develop an SSPP to provide a basis of 
understanding between the contractor and the Program Manager (PM) on how the safety hazard 
management effort will be integrated into the SE process. The SSPP shall include the following 
sections:
blocks.normalized_text:
102.2 Task description. The contractor shall develop an SSPP to provide a basis of
understanding between the contractor and the Program Manager (PM) on how the safety hazard
management effort will be integrated into the SE process. The SSPP shall include the following
sections:

blocks.text:
102.2.1 Scope and objectives. The SSPP shall describe, at a minimum: (1) the scope of 
the effort in terms of the system and its life-cycle, (2) the overall approach for accomplishing the 
General Requirements in Section 4 and other contractually required tasks, (3) integration of those 
efforts into SE and other Program Office management processes in order to support overall 
program objectives, and (4) resource requirements (funding, qualified personnel, and tools) to 
execute the SSPP. This Section shall account for all contractual hazard management 
requirements by providing a matrix that correlates these contractual requirements to the 
location(s) in the SSPP where each requirement is addressed.

efforts into SE and other Program Office management processes in order to support overall

execute the SSPP. This Section shall account for all contractual hazard management
requirements by providing a matrix that correlates these contractual requirements to the
location(s) in the SSPP where each requirement is addressed.

blocks.text: 102.2.2 SSPP interfaces. The SSPP shall:
blocks.normalized_text: 102.2.2 SSPP interfaces. The SSPP shall:

blocks.text:
(2) Other involved disciplines (e.g., logistics, maintainability, quality assurance, 
reliability, human factors engineering, transportability engineering, and medical support (health 
hazard assessments)).

reliability, human factors engineering, transportability engineering, and medical support (health
hazard assessments)).

blocks.text:
a. The organization or function of the system safety efforts within the SE process. Use 
charts to show the organizational and functional relationships and lines of communication.

blocks.text:
MIL-STD-882E 
 w/CHANGE 1
blocks.normalized_text:
MIL-STD-882E
 w/CHANGE 1

blocks.text:
SSPP will identify responsibility and authority of each person and organizational unit involved in 
executing each of the contractual system safety requirements. The SSPP will also identify key 
personnel, and provide a summary of the qualifications and credentials of the key system safety 
personnel. The SSPP will describe how and when the Contractor shall notify the Government 
prior to changes of key system safety personnel.
blocks.normalized_text:
SSPP will identify responsibility and authority of each person and organizational unit involved in
executing each of the contractual system safety requirements. The SSPP will also identify key
personnel, and provide a summary of the qualifications and credentials of the key system safety
personnel. The SSPP will describe how and when the Contractor shall notify the Government
prior to changes of key system safety personnel.

blocks.text:
c. The procedures the contractor will use to integrate system-level and System-of- 
Systems (SoS) level hazard management efforts to the extent covered in the contract. These will 
include:
blocks.normalized_text:
c. The procedures the contractor will use to integrate system-level and System-of-
Systems (SoS) level hazard management efforts to the extent covered in the contract. These will
include:

blocks.text:
(1) Defining the roles of each associate contractor and subcontractor (and suppliers and 
vendors as applicable) to integrate safety requirements for the total system.
blocks.normalized_text:
(1) Defining the roles of each associate contractor and subcontractor (and suppliers and
vendors as applicable) to integrate safety requirements for the total system.

blocks.text:
(2) Defining the safety interfaces between each associate contractor and subcontractor 
(and suppliers and vendors as applicable), e.g. integrating hazard analyses.
blocks.normalized_text:
(2) Defining the safety interfaces between each associate contractor and subcontractor
(and suppliers and vendors as applicable), e.g. integrating hazard analyses.

blocks.text:
(3) Establishing Integrated Product Teams (IPTs) or Working Groups (WGs) with 
representatives from each associate contractor and subcontractor (and suppliers and vendors as 
applicable).
blocks.normalized_text:
(3) Establishing Integrated Product Teams (IPTs) or Working Groups (WGs) with
representatives from each associate contractor and subcontractor (and suppliers and vendors as
applicable).

blocks.text: (4) Describing any specific SoS integration roles and responsibilities.
blocks.normalized_text: (4) Describing any specific SoS integration roles and responsibilities.

blocks.text: (5) Integrating hardware and software provided by the Government.
blocks.normalized_text: (5) Integrating hardware and software provided by the Government.

blocks.text: (6) Assigning requirements to action organizations and subcontractors.
blocks.normalized_text: (6) Assigning requirements to action organizations and subcontractors.

blocks.text: (7) Coordinating subcontractor system safety engineering efforts.
blocks.normalized_text: (7) Coordinating subcontractor system safety engineering efforts.

blocks.text: (8) Facilitating safety reviews.
blocks.normalized_text: (8) Facilitating safety reviews.

blocks.text:
(9) Recommending mitigation measures; assessing feasibility, cost, and effectiveness of 
the measures; and allocating implementation responsibility to associate contractors and 
subcontractors.
blocks.normalized_text:
(9) Recommending mitigation measures; assessing feasibility, cost, and effectiveness of
the measures; and allocating implementation responsibility to associate contractors and
subcontractors.

blocks.text: (10) Reporting on program safety status and metrics.
blocks.normalized_text: (10) Reporting on program safety status and metrics.

blocks.text:
(11) Describing procedures for addressing safety issues between associate contractors 
and subcontractors.
blocks.normalized_text:
(11) Describing procedures for addressing safety issues between associate contractors
and subcontractors.

blocks.text:
d. The process through which contractor management decisions will be made including 
timely notification of hazards with Catastrophic and Critical severity levels, as well as High and 
Serious risks to the Government; determining actions necessary in the event of mishaps, 
incidents, or malfunctions; and requesting waivers for safety requirements and program 
deviations.
blocks.normalized_text:

blocks.text:
MIL-STD-882E 
 w/CHANGE 1
blocks.normalized_text:
MIL-STD-882E
 w/CHANGE 1

blocks.text:
a. Provide a schedule of system safety activities including required inputs and outputs, 
and start and completion dates that support the SE process.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. Provide a schedule of system safety activities including required inputs and outputs,
and start and completion dates that support the SE process.

blocks.text:
b. Relate the system safety activities to integrated system-level activities (e.g., design 
analyses, tests, and demonstrations), technical reviews, program reviews, and major program 
milestones by recommending their inclusion in the Integrated Master Schedule (IMS).
blocks.normalized_text:
b. Relate the system safety activities to integrated system-level activities (e.g., design
analyses, tests, and demonstrations), technical reviews, program reviews, and major program
milestones by recommending their inclusion in the Integrated Master Schedule (IMS).

blocks.text:
c. Identify the schedules for subsystem, component, and software activities applicable to 
the system safety activities but specified in other engineering studies and development efforts.
blocks.normalized_text:
c. Identify the schedules for subsystem, component, and software activities applicable to
the system safety activities but specified in other engineering studies and development efforts.

blocks.text:
d. Include a schedule of technical meetings between associate contractors and 
subcontractors to discuss, review, and integrate the safety effort.
blocks.normalized_text:
d. Include a schedule of technical meetings between associate contractors and
subcontractors to discuss, review, and integrate the safety effort.

blocks.text: 102.2.5 General safety requirements and criteria. The SSPP shall:
blocks.normalized_text: 102.2.5 General safety requirements and criteria. The SSPP shall:

blocks.text:
a. List the standards and system specifications containing safety requirements that the 
contractor shall use in the execution of the contract. Include titles, dates, and where applicable, 
paragraph numbers.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. List the standards and system specifications containing safety requirements that the
contractor shall use in the execution of the contract. Include titles, dates, and where applicable,
paragraph numbers.

blocks.text:
b. Describe general engineering requirements and design criteria for safety risk 
management during system design and development.
blocks.normalized_text:
b. Describe general engineering requirements and design criteria for safety risk
management during system design and development.

blocks.text:
c. Identify safety risk management requirements, to include procedures, for test, 
operations and support, and disposal.
blocks.normalized_text:
c. Identify safety risk management requirements, to include procedures, for test,
operations and support, and disposal.

blocks.text:
d. Describe the method for ensuring flow-down of hazard identification and mitigation 
functions as well as associated requirements to subcontractors/suppliers.
blocks.normalized_text:
d. Describe the method for ensuring flow-down of hazard identification and mitigation
functions as well as associated requirements to subcontractors/suppliers.

blocks.text: 102.2.6 Hazard analysis. At a minimum, the SSPP shall:
blocks.normalized_text: 102.2.6 Hazard analysis. At a minimum, the SSPP shall:

blocks.text:
a. Describe the processes for hazard identification, risk assessment, risk mitigation, risk 
communication, and support to risk acceptance.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. Describe the processes for hazard identification, risk assessment, risk mitigation, risk
communication, and support to risk acceptance.

blocks.text:
(1) For hazard identification, the SSPP shall describe the systematic identification 
process that evaluates the system throughout its life-cycle. This evaluation should include as a 
minimum system hardware and software, system interfaces (to include human interfaces), the 
intended use or application and operational environment, and disposal.
blocks.normalized_text:
(1) For hazard identification, the SSPP shall describe the systematic identification
process that evaluates the system throughout its life-cycle. This evaluation should include as a
minimum system hardware and software, system interfaces (to include human interfaces), the
intended use or application and operational environment, and disposal.

blocks.text:
(2) For risk assessment, the SSPP shall list the severity categories, probability levels, and 
Risk Assessment Codes (RACs) that shall be followed. The definitions in Tables I and II, and 
the RACs in Table III shall be used, unless tailored alternative definitions and/or a tailored 
matrix are formally approved in accordance with Department of Defense (DoD) Component 
policy.
blocks.normalized_text:
(2) For risk assessment, the SSPP shall list the severity categories, probability levels, and
Risk Assessment Codes (RACs) that shall be followed. The definitions in Tables I and II, and
the RACs in Table III shall be used, unless tailored alternative definitions and/or a tailored
matrix are formally approved in accordance with Department of Defense (DoD) Component
policy.

blocks.text:
MIL-STD-882E 
 w/CHANGE 1
blocks.normalized_text:
MIL-STD-882E
 w/CHANGE 1

blocks.text:
(3) For risk mitigation, the SSPP shall describe how decisions will be made within the 
overall SE process. The SSPP shall emphasize that the goal should always be to eliminate the 
hazard if possible. When a hazard cannot be eliminated, the SSPP should describe the process 
for determining how the associated risk could be reduced to the lowest acceptable level within 
the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system safety design order of 
precedence described in Section 4 of this Standard. SE process decisions on which mitigations 
to pursue will be the result of trade-off discussions between the involved technical disciplines.
blocks.normalized_text:
(3) For risk mitigation, the SSPP shall describe how decisions will be made within the
overall SE process. The SSPP shall emphasize that the goal should always be to eliminate the
hazard if possible. When a hazard cannot be eliminated, the SSPP should describe the process
for determining how the associated risk could be reduced to the lowest acceptable level within
the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system safety design order of
precedence described in Section 4 of this Standard. SE process decisions on which mitigations
to pursue will be the result of trade-off discussions between the involved technical disciplines.

blocks.text:
(4) For risk acceptance, the SSPP shall describe the plan to address Government risk 
acceptance to include the procedures for communicating to the Government that a risk 
acceptance decision is required and providing the risk assessment documentation. In addition, 
the plan should include the procedures the Government has provided on how the Government 
will communicate to the Contractor the results of the proposed risk acceptance decision. In 
accordance with applicable Department of Defense Instruction (DoDI) 5000 series, the 
Government may have to accept an event risk at multiple points in the life-cycle.
blocks.normalized_text:
(4) For risk acceptance, the SSPP shall describe the plan to address Government risk
acceptance to include the procedures for communicating to the Government that a risk
acceptance decision is required and providing the risk assessment documentation. In addition,
the plan should include the procedures the Government has provided on how the Government
will communicate to the Contractor the results of the proposed risk acceptance decision. In
accordance with applicable Department of Defense Instruction (DoDI) 5000 series, the
Government may have to accept an event risk at multiple points in the life-cycle.

blocks.text:
b. Describe the approach for applying safety risk management to the use of Commercial- 
Off-the-Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Non-Developmental Item (NDI), 
Government-Furnished Equipment (GFE), and Government-Furnished Information (GFI).
blocks.normalized_text:
b. Describe the approach for applying safety risk management to the use of Commercial-
Off-the-Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Non-Developmental Item (NDI),
Government-Furnished Equipment (GFE), and Government-Furnished Information (GFI).

blocks.text:
c. Describe closed-loop procedures for tracking and reporting identified hazards and 
associated risks, including those involving COTS, GOTS, NDI, GFE, and GFI. Include a 
detailed description of the Hazard Tracking System (HTS).
blocks.normalized_text:
c. Describe closed-loop procedures for tracking and reporting identified hazards and
associated risks, including those involving COTS, GOTS, NDI, GFE, and GFI. Include a
detailed description of the Hazard Tracking System (HTS).

blocks.text:
d. Describe the process for determining whether a qualitative or quantitative risk 
assessment is appropriate for a given hazard.
blocks.normalized_text:
d. Describe the process for determining whether a qualitative or quantitative risk
assessment is appropriate for a given hazard.

blocks.text:
e. Identify the hazard analyses to be performed (e.g., Preliminary Hazard Analysis 
[PHA], Subsystem Hazard Analysis [SSHA]), analytical techniques to be used (e.g., Fault Tree 
Analysis [FTA], Failure Modes and Effects Criticality Analysis [FMECA]), and documentation 
of the results in the HTS.
blocks.normalized_text:
e. Identify the hazard analyses to be performed (e.g., Preliminary Hazard Analysis
[PHA], Subsystem Hazard Analysis [SSHA]), analytical techniques to be used (e.g., Fault Tree
Analysis [FTA], Failure Modes and Effects Criticality Analysis [FMECA]), and documentation
of the results in the HTS.

blocks.text:
f. Identify the scope of each analysis, integration of associate contractor and 
subcontractor hazard analyses with overall system hazard analyses, and the depth within the 
system that each analytical technique will be used.
blocks.normalized_text:
f. Identify the scope of each analysis, integration of associate contractor and
subcontractor hazard analyses with overall system hazard analyses, and the depth within the
system that each analytical technique will be used.

blocks.text:
g. When conducting SoS hazard analyses, the plan shall describe how analysis of the 
integrated system design, operations, and the interfaces between the products of each associate 
contractor or subcontractor and the end item will be executed. Data or analyses provided by 
associate contractors and subcontractors (and suppliers and vendors as applicable) shall be used 
in the conduct of this effort.
blocks.normalized_text:
g. When conducting SoS hazard analyses, the plan shall describe how analysis of the
integrated system design, operations, and the interfaces between the products of each associate
contractor or subcontractor and the end item will be executed. Data or analyses provided by
associate contractors and subcontractors (and suppliers and vendors as applicable) shall be used
in the conduct of this effort.

blocks.text:
h. Describe the efforts to identify and control hazards associated with materials used 
during the system’s life-cycle.
blocks.normalized_text:
h. Describe the efforts to identify and control hazards associated with materials used
during the system’s life-cycle.

of the results in the HTS.

blocks.text:
TASK 103 
HAZARD MANAGEMENT PLAN
blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 103
HAZARD MANAGEMENT PLAN

blocks.text:
TASK 103 
HAZARD MANAGEMENT PLAN
blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 103
HAZARD MANAGEMENT PLAN

blocks.text: TASK 103
blocks.normalized_text: TASK 103

blocks.text:
103.1 Purpose. Task 103 is to develop a Hazard Management Plan (HMP) that documents a 
standard, generic system safety methodology for the identification, classification, and mitigation 
of hazards as part of the overall Systems Engineering (SE) process. The HMP should be an 
integral part of the Systems Engineering Management Plan (SEMP). The HMP shall detail the 
tasks and activities that are required to implement a systematic approach of hazard analysis, risk 
assessment, and risk management. The goal should always be to eliminate the hazard if possible. 
When a hazard cannot be eliminated, the associated risk should be reduced to the lowest 
acceptable level within the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system 
safety design order of precedence.
blocks.normalized_text:
103.1 Purpose. Task 103 is to develop a Hazard Management Plan (HMP) that documents a
standard, generic system safety methodology for the identification, classification, and mitigation
of hazards as part of the overall Systems Engineering (SE) process. The HMP should be an
integral part of the Systems Engineering Management Plan (SEMP). The HMP shall detail the
tasks and activities that are required to implement a systematic approach of hazard analysis, risk
assessment, and risk management. The goal should always be to eliminate the hazard if possible.
When a hazard cannot be eliminated, the associated risk should be reduced to the lowest
acceptable level within the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system
safety design order of precedence.

blocks.text:
103.2 Task description. The contractor shall develop an HMP to provide a basis of 
understanding between the contractor and the Program Manager (PM) on how the hazard 
management effort will be integrated into the SE process. The HMP shall include the following 
sections:
blocks.normalized_text:
103.2 Task description. The contractor shall develop an HMP to provide a basis of
understanding between the contractor and the Program Manager (PM) on how the hazard
management effort will be integrated into the SE process. The HMP shall include the following
sections:

blocks.text:
103.2.1 Scope and objectives. The HMP shall describe, at a minimum: (1) the scope of 
the effort in terms of the system and its life-cycle, (2) the overall approach for accomplishing the 
General Requirements in Section 4 and other contractually required tasks, (3) integration of those 
efforts into SE and other Program Office management processes in order to support overall 
program objectives, and (4) resource requirements (funding, qualified personnel, and tools) to 
execute the HMP. This Section shall account for all contractual hazard management 
requirements by providing a matrix that correlates these contractual requirements to the 
location(s) in the HMP where each requirement is addressed.

efforts into SE and other Program Office management processes in order to support overall
program objectives, and (4) resource requirements (funding, qualified personnel, and tools) to
execute the HMP. This Section shall account for all contractual hazard management
requirements by providing a matrix that correlates these contractual requirements to the
location(s) in the HMP where each requirement is addressed.

blocks.text: b. Describe the HMP interfaces between:

blocks.text:
(2) Functional disciplines using the system safety methodology as described in Section 4 
of this Standard (e.g., system safety, range safety, fire protection engineering, environmental 
engineering, explosive and ordnance safety, chemical and biological safety, directed energy, 
laser and radio-frequency safety, software system safety, industrial hygiene, occupational health, 
and Human Systems Integration (HSI)).

and Human Systems Integration (HSI)).

blocks.text:
(3) Other involved disciplines (e.g., logistics, maintainability, quality control, reliability,

blocks.text:
MIL-STD-882E 
 w/CHANGE 1
blocks.normalized_text:
MIL-STD-882E
 w/CHANGE 1

blocks.text:
a. The organization or function of the HMP efforts within the SE process. Use charts to 
show the organizational and functional relationships and lines of communication.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. The organization or function of the HMP efforts within the SE process. Use charts to
show the organizational and functional relationships and lines of communication.

blocks.text:
b. The staffing (manpower loading and schedule) of the HMP efforts by each of the 
involved functional disciplines and organizational units for the duration of the contract. The 
HMP will identify responsibility and authority of each person and organizational unit involved in 
executing each of the contractual HMP requirements. The HMP will also identify key personnel, 
and provide a summary of their qualifications and credentials. The HMP will describe how and 
when the Contractor shall notify the Government prior to changes to key personnel 
implementing the HMP .
blocks.normalized_text:
b. The staffing (manpower loading and schedule) of the HMP efforts by each of the
involved functional disciplines and organizational units for the duration of the contract. The
HMP will identify responsibility and authority of each person and organizational unit involved in
executing each of the contractual HMP requirements. The HMP will also identify key personnel,
and provide a summary of their qualifications and credentials. The HMP will describe how and
when the Contractor shall notify the Government prior to changes to key personnel
implementing the HMP .

blocks.text:
c. The procedures the contractor will use to integrate system-level and System-of- 
Systems (SoS) level hazard management efforts to the extent covered in the contract. These will 
include:
blocks.normalized_text:
c. The procedures the contractor will use to integrate system-level and System-of-
Systems (SoS) level hazard management efforts to the extent covered in the contract. These will
include:

blocks.text:
(1) Defining the roles of each associate contractor and subcontractor (and suppliers and 
vendors as applicable) to integrate HMP requirements for the total system.
blocks.normalized_text:
(1) Defining the roles of each associate contractor and subcontractor (and suppliers and
vendors as applicable) to integrate HMP requirements for the total system.

blocks.text:
(2) Defining the HMP interfaces between each associate contractor and subcontractor 
(and suppliers and vendors as applicable), e.g. integrating hazard analyses.
blocks.normalized_text:
(2) Defining the HMP interfaces between each associate contractor and subcontractor
(and suppliers and vendors as applicable), e.g. integrating hazard analyses.

blocks.text:
(3) Establishing Integrated Product Teams (IPTs) or Working Groups (WGs) with 
representatives from each associate contractor and subcontractor (and suppliers and vendors as 
applicable).
blocks.normalized_text:
(3) Establishing Integrated Product Teams (IPTs) or Working Groups (WGs) with
representatives from each associate contractor and subcontractor (and suppliers and vendors as
applicable).

blocks.text: (4) Describing any specific SoS integration roles and responsibilities.
blocks.normalized_text: (4) Describing any specific SoS integration roles and responsibilities.

blocks.text: (5) Integrating hardware and software provided by the Government.
blocks.normalized_text: (5) Integrating hardware and software provided by the Government.

blocks.text: (6) Assigning requirements to action organizations and subcontractors.
blocks.normalized_text: (6) Assigning requirements to action organizations and subcontractors.

blocks.text: (7) Coordinating subcontractor HMP engineering efforts.
blocks.normalized_text: (7) Coordinating subcontractor HMP engineering efforts.

blocks.text:
(8) Recommending mitigation measures; assessing feasibility, cost, and effectiveness of 
the measures; and allocating implementation responsibility to associate contractors and 
subcontractors.
blocks.normalized_text:
(8) Recommending mitigation measures; assessing feasibility, cost, and effectiveness of
the measures; and allocating implementation responsibility to associate contractors and
subcontractors.

blocks.text: (9) Reporting on hazard management status and metrics.
blocks.normalized_text: (9) Reporting on hazard management status and metrics.

blocks.text:
(10) Describing procedures for addressing hazard management issues between associate 
contractors and subcontractors.
blocks.normalized_text:
(10) Describing procedures for addressing hazard management issues between associate
contractors and subcontractors.

blocks.text:
d. The process through which contractor management decisions will be made including 
timely notification of High and Serious risks to the Government; determining actions necessary 
in the event of mishaps, incidents, or malfunctions; and requesting waivers for hazard 
management requirements and program deviations.
blocks.normalized_text:
d. The process through which contractor management decisions will be made including
timely notification of High and Serious risks to the Government; determining actions necessary
in the event of mishaps, incidents, or malfunctions; and requesting waivers for hazard
management requirements and program deviations.

blocks.text:
MIL-STD-882E 
 w/CHANGE 1
blocks.normalized_text:
MIL-STD-882E
 w/CHANGE 1

blocks.text: 103.2.4 Milestones. The HMP shall, at a minimum:
blocks.normalized_text: 103.2.4 Milestones. The HMP shall, at a minimum:

blocks.text:
a. Provide a schedule of hazard management activities including required inputs and 
outputs, and start and completion dates that support the SE process.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. Provide a schedule of hazard management activities including required inputs and
outputs, and start and completion dates that support the SE process.

blocks.text:
b. Relate the hazard management activities to integrated system-level activities (e.g., 
design analyses, tests, and demonstrations), technical reviews, program reviews, and major 
program milestones by recommending their inclusion in the Integrated Master Schedule (IMS).
blocks.normalized_text:
b. Relate the hazard management activities to integrated system-level activities (e.g.,
design analyses, tests, and demonstrations), technical reviews, program reviews, and major
program milestones by recommending their inclusion in the Integrated Master Schedule (IMS).

blocks.text:
c. Identify the schedules for subsystem, component, and software activities applicable to 
the hazard management activities but specified in other engineering studies and development 
efforts.
blocks.normalized_text:
c. Identify the schedules for subsystem, component, and software activities applicable to
the hazard management activities but specified in other engineering studies and development
efforts.

blocks.text:
d. Include a schedule of technical meetings between associate contractors and 
subcontractors to discuss, review, and integrate the safety effort.
blocks.normalized_text:
d. Include a schedule of technical meetings between associate contractors and
subcontractors to discuss, review, and integrate the safety effort.

blocks.text: 103.2.5 General HMP requirements and criteria. The HMP shall:
blocks.normalized_text: 103.2.5 General HMP requirements and criteria. The HMP shall:

blocks.text:
a. List the standards and system specifications containing hazard management 
requirements that the contractor shall use in the execution of the contract. Include titles, dates, 
and where applicable, paragraph numbers.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. List the standards and system specifications containing hazard management
requirements that the contractor shall use in the execution of the contract. Include titles, dates,
and where applicable, paragraph numbers.

blocks.text:
b. Describe general engineering requirements and design criteria for hazard management 
during system design and development.
blocks.normalized_text:
b. Describe general engineering requirements and design criteria for hazard management
during system design and development.

blocks.text:
c. Identify hazard management requirements, to include procedures, for test, operations 
and support, and disposal.
blocks.normalized_text:
c. Identify hazard management requirements, to include procedures, for test, operations
and support, and disposal.

blocks.text:
d. Describe the method for ensuring flow-down of hazard identification and mitigation 
functions as well as associated requirements to subcontractors/suppliers.
blocks.normalized_text:
d. Describe the method for ensuring flow-down of hazard identification and mitigation
functions as well as associated requirements to subcontractors/suppliers.

blocks.text: 103.2.6 Hazard analysis. At a minimum, the HMP shall:
blocks.normalized_text: 103.2.6 Hazard analysis. At a minimum, the HMP shall:

blocks.text:
a. Describe the processes for hazard identification, risk assessment, risk mitigation, risk 
communication, and support to risk acceptance.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. Describe the processes for hazard identification, risk assessment, risk mitigation, risk
communication, and support to risk acceptance.

blocks.text:
(1) For hazard identification, the HMP shall describe the systematic identification 
process that evaluates the system throughout its life-cycle. This evaluation should include as a 
minimum system hardware and software, system interfaces (to include human interfaces), the 
intended use or application and operational environment, and disposal.
blocks.normalized_text:
(1) For hazard identification, the HMP shall describe the systematic identification
process that evaluates the system throughout its life-cycle. This evaluation should include as a
minimum system hardware and software, system interfaces (to include human interfaces), the
intended use or application and operational environment, and disposal.

blocks.text:
(2) For risk assessment, the HMP shall list the severity categories, probability levels, and 
Risk Assessment Codes (RACs) that shall be followed. The definitions in Tables I and II, and 
the RACs in Table III shall be used, unless tailored alternative definitions and/or a tailored
blocks.normalized_text:
(2) For risk assessment, the HMP shall list the severity categories, probability levels, and
Risk Assessment Codes (RACs) that shall be followed. The definitions in Tables I and II, and
the RACs in Table III shall be used, unless tailored alternative definitions and/or a tailored

MIL-STD-882E 
 w/CHANGE 1
blocks.normalized_text:
MIL-STD-882E
 w/CHANGE 1

blocks.text:
matrix are formally approved in accordance with Department of Defense (DoD) Component 
policy.
blocks.normalized_text:
matrix are formally approved in accordance with Department of Defense (DoD) Component
policy.

blocks.text:
(3) For risk mitigation, the HMP shall describe how decisions will be made within the 
overall SE process. The HMP shall emphasize that the goal should always be to eliminate the 
hazard if possible. When a hazard cannot be eliminated, the HMP should describe the process 
for determining how the associated risk could be reduced to the lowest acceptable level within 
the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system safety design order of 
precedence described in Section 4 of this Standard. SE process decisions on which mitigations 
to pursue will be the result of trade-off discussions between the involved technical disciplines.
blocks.normalized_text:
(3) For risk mitigation, the HMP shall describe how decisions will be made within the
overall SE process. The HMP shall emphasize that the goal should always be to eliminate the
hazard if possible. When a hazard cannot be eliminated, the HMP should describe the process
for determining how the associated risk could be reduced to the lowest acceptable level within
the constraints of cost, schedule, and performance by applying the system safety design order of
precedence described in Section 4 of this Standard. SE process decisions on which mitigations
to pursue will be the result of trade-off discussions between the involved technical disciplines.

blocks.text:
(4) For risk acceptance, the HMP shall describe the plan to address Government risk 
acceptance to include the procedures for communicating to the Government that a risk 
acceptance decision is required and providing the risk assessment documentation. In addition, 
the plan should include the procedures the Government has provided on how the Government 
will communicate to the Contractor the results of the proposed risk acceptance decision. In 
accordance with applicable Department of Defense Instruction (DoDI) 5000 series, the 
Government may have to accept an event risk at multiple points in the life-cycle.
blocks.normalized_text:
(4) For risk acceptance, the HMP shall describe the plan to address Government risk
acceptance to include the procedures for communicating to the Government that a risk
acceptance decision is required and providing the risk assessment documentation. In addition,
the plan should include the procedures the Government has provided on how the Government
will communicate to the Contractor the results of the proposed risk acceptance decision. In
accordance with applicable Department of Defense Instruction (DoDI) 5000 series, the
Government may have to accept an event risk at multiple points in the life-cycle.

blocks.text:
b. Describe the approach for applying safety risk management to the use of Commercial- 
Off-the-Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Non-Developmental Item (NDI), 
Government-Furnished Equipment (GFE), and Government-Furnished Information (GFI).
blocks.normalized_text:
b. Describe the approach for applying safety risk management to the use of Commercial-
Off-the-Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Non-Developmental Item (NDI),
Government-Furnished Equipment (GFE), and Government-Furnished Information (GFI).

blocks.text:
c. Describe closed-loop procedures for tracking and reporting identified hazards and 
associated risks, including those involving COTS, GOTS, NDI, GFE, and GFI. Include a 
detailed description of the Hazard Tracking System (HTS).
blocks.normalized_text:
c. Describe closed-loop procedures for tracking and reporting identified hazards and
associated risks, including those involving COTS, GOTS, NDI, GFE, and GFI. Include a
detailed description of the Hazard Tracking System (HTS).

blocks.text:
d. Describe the process for determining whether a qualitative or quantitative risk 
assessment is appropriate for a given hazard.
blocks.normalized_text:
d. Describe the process for determining whether a qualitative or quantitative risk
assessment is appropriate for a given hazard.

blocks.text:
e. Identify the hazard analyses to be performed (e.g., Preliminary Hazard Analysis 
[PHA], Subsystem Hazard Analysis [SSHA]), analytical techniques to be used (e.g., Fault Tree 
Analysis [FTA], Failure Modes and Effects Criticality Analysis [FMECA]), and documentation 
of the results in the HTS.
blocks.normalized_text:
e. Identify the hazard analyses to be performed (e.g., Preliminary Hazard Analysis
[PHA], Subsystem Hazard Analysis [SSHA]), analytical techniques to be used (e.g., Fault Tree
Analysis [FTA], Failure Modes and Effects Criticality Analysis [FMECA]), and documentation
of the results in the HTS.

blocks.text:
f. Identify the scope of each analysis, integration of associate contractor and 
subcontractor hazard analyses with overall system hazard analyses, and the depth within the 
system that each analytical technique will be used (e.g., system level, subsystem level, 
component level).
blocks.normalized_text:
f. Identify the scope of each analysis, integration of associate contractor and
subcontractor hazard analyses with overall system hazard analyses, and the depth within the
system that each analytical technique will be used (e.g., system level, subsystem level,
component level).

blocks.text:
g. When conducting SoS hazard analyses, the plan shall describe how analysis of the 
integrated system design, operations, and the interfaces between the products of each associate 
contractor or subcontractor and the end item will be executed. Data or analyses provided by 
associate contractors and subcontractors (and suppliers and vendors as applicable) shall be used 
in the conduct of this effort.
blocks.normalized_text:
g. When conducting SoS hazard analyses, the plan shall describe how analysis of the
integrated system design, operations, and the interfaces between the products of each associate
contractor or subcontractor and the end item will be executed. Data or analyses provided by
associate contractors and subcontractors (and suppliers and vendors as applicable) shall be used
in the conduct of this effort.

text_blocks.normalized_text:
c. Describe closed-loop procedures for tracking and reporting identified hazards and
associated risks, including those involving COTS, GOTS, NDI, GFE, and GFI. Include a
detailed description of the Hazard Tracking System (HTS).

text_blocks.text:
d. Describe the process for determining whether a qualitative or quantitative risk 
assessment is appropriate for a given hazard.
text_blocks.normalized_text:
d. Describe the process for determining whether a qualitative or quantitative risk
assessment is appropriate for a given hazard.

text_blocks.text:
e. Identify the hazard analyses to be performed (e.g., Preliminary Hazard Analysis 
[PHA], Subsystem Hazard Analysis [SSHA]), analytical techniques to be used (e.g., Fault Tree 
Analysis [FTA], Failure Modes and Effects Criticality Analysis [FMECA]), and documentation 
of the results in the HTS.
text_blocks.normalized_text:
e. Identify the hazard analyses to be performed (e.g., Preliminary Hazard Analysis
[PHA], Subsystem Hazard Analysis [SSHA]), analytical techniques to be used (e.g., Fault Tree
Analysis [FTA], Failure Modes and Effects Criticality Analysis [FMECA]), and documentation
of the results in the HTS.

text_blocks.text:
f. Identify the scope of each analysis, integration of associate contractor and 
subcontractor hazard analyses with overall system hazard analyses, and the depth within the 
system that each analytical technique will be used (e.g., system level, subsystem level, 
component level).
text_blocks.normalized_text:
f. Identify the scope of each analysis, integration of associate contractor and
subcontractor hazard analyses with overall system hazard analyses, and the depth within the
system that each analytical technique will be used (e.g., system level, subsystem level,
component level).

text_blocks.text:
g. When conducting SoS hazard analyses, the plan shall describe how analysis of the 
integrated system design, operations, and the interfaces between the products of each associate 
contractor or subcontractor and the end item will be executed. Data or analyses provided by 
associate contractors and subcontractors (and suppliers and vendors as applicable) shall be used 
in the conduct of this effort.
text_blocks.normalized_text:
g. When conducting SoS hazard analyses, the plan shall describe how analysis of the
integrated system design, operations, and the interfaces between the products of each associate
contractor or subcontractor and the end item will be executed. Data or analyses provided by
associate contractors and subcontractors (and suppliers and vendors as applicable) shall be used
in the conduct of this effort.

blocks.text: 103.2.7 Supporting data. At a minimum, the HMP shall:
blocks.normalized_text: 103.2.7 Supporting data. At a minimum, the HMP shall:

blocks.text:
a. Describe the approach for collecting and processing pertinent hazard, mishap, and 
lessons learned data. This should include both historical data from similar or legacy systems 
used to assist in hazard identification and associated risk assessment, and current system data, 
e.g., the HTS.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. Describe the approach for collecting and processing pertinent hazard, mishap, and
lessons learned data. This should include both historical data from similar or legacy systems
used to assist in hazard identification and associated risk assessment, and current system data,
e.g., the HTS.

blocks.text:
b. Identify all documents or other media incorporating hazard management data by title, 
contract number, date(s) of delivery, and proposed means of delivery (hard copy, electronic, or 
real-time access) intended to be delivered to the Government under this contract, including 
documents or other media with other than unlimited rights for the Government. At a minimum, 
deliverable data shall include HTS data provided during contract execution and at contract 
closeout.
blocks.normalized_text:
b. Identify all documents or other media incorporating hazard management data by title,
contract number, date(s) of delivery, and proposed means of delivery (hard copy, electronic, or
real-time access) intended to be delivered to the Government under this contract, including
documents or other media with other than unlimited rights for the Government. At a minimum,
deliverable data shall include HTS data provided during contract execution and at contract
closeout.

blocks.text:
103.2.8 Verification and validation. At a minimum, the HMP shall document how the 
hazard management effort will:
blocks.normalized_text:
103.2.8 Verification and validation. At a minimum, the HMP shall document how the
hazard management effort will:

blocks.text:
a. Verify, validate, and document effectiveness of mitigation measures in reducing risk 
through test, analysis, inspection, etc.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. Verify, validate, and document effectiveness of mitigation measures in reducing risk
through test, analysis, inspection, etc.

blocks.text:
b. Verify, validate, and document that hardware, software, and procedures comply with 
identified hazard management requirements.
blocks.normalized_text:
b. Verify, validate, and document that hardware, software, and procedures comply with
identified hazard management requirements.

blocks.text:
c. Identify requirements for certifications, independent review board evaluations, and 
special testing (e.g., insensitive munitions tests, Hazards of Electromagnetic Radiation to 
Ordnance (HERO), Electrostatic Discharge (ESD), and render-safe /emergency disposal 
procedures).
blocks.normalized_text:
c. Identify requirements for certifications, independent review board evaluations, and
special testing (e.g., insensitive munitions tests, Hazards of Electromagnetic Radiation to
Ordnance (HERO), Electrostatic Discharge (ESD), and render-safe /emergency disposal
procedures).

blocks.text:
d. Ensure procedures are in place to transmit verification and validation information to 
the Government.
blocks.normalized_text:
d. Ensure procedures are in place to transmit verification and validation information to
the Government.

WGs. Such participation shall include, but is not limited to, the following activities:

blocks.text: a. Summarizing hazard analyses and the status of associated risk reduction efforts.

blocks.text: b. Identifying issues or problems associated with risk mitigations.

and associated reduction of risks.

blocks.text:
d. Presenting incident (especially mishaps and malfunctions of the system being 
acquired) assessment results, including recommendations and actions taken to prevent 
recurrences.

recurrences.

blocks.text: e. Responding to action items assigned by the designated IPT or WG.

blocks.text:
108.1 Purpose. Task 108 is to implement a Hazardous Materials Management Plan (HMMP) 
which shall be made available to the Government on request. Hazardous Material (HAZMAT) 
management is an integral part of the risk management effort within the program’s System 
Engineering (SE) process using this Standard's methodology.
blocks.normalized_text:
108.1 Purpose. Task 108 is to implement a Hazardous Materials Management Plan (HMMP)
which shall be made available to the Government on request. Hazardous Material (HAZMAT)
management is an integral part of the risk management effort within the program’s System
Engineering (SE) process using this Standard's methodology.

blocks.text:
108.2 Task description. The contractor shall use the HMMP to define contractor roles, 
responsibilities, and procedures needed to accomplish HAZMAT management and tracking. The 
plan shall account for contractually required HAZMAT management tasks and responsibilities. 
At a minimum, the HMMP shall identify the following:
blocks.normalized_text:
108.2 Task description. The contractor shall use the HMMP to define contractor roles,
responsibilities, and procedures needed to accomplish HAZMAT management and tracking. The
plan shall account for contractually required HAZMAT management tasks and responsibilities.
At a minimum, the HMMP shall identify the following:

blocks.text:
a. The processes to properly identify, analyze, and control HAZMAT risks to protect 
human health, safety, and the environment, as well as to support end user needs.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. The processes to properly identify, analyze, and control HAZMAT risks to protect
human health, safety, and the environment, as well as to support end user needs.

blocks.text: b. Procedures for tracking and reporting HAZMAT.
blocks.normalized_text: b. Procedures for tracking and reporting HAZMAT.

blocks.text:
108.2.1 HAZMAT identification. A HAZMAT is defined as any item or substance that, 
due to its chemical, physical, toxicological, or biological nature, could cause harm to people, 
equipment, or the environment.
blocks.normalized_text:
108.2.1 HAZMAT identification. A HAZMAT is defined as any item or substance that,
due to its chemical, physical, toxicological, or biological nature, could cause harm to people,
equipment, or the environment.

blocks.text:

the delivered hardware and/or required for system operation and support, categorized as 
prohibited, restricted, or tracked, will be mutually agreed upon by the Government and 
contractor.
blocks.normalized_text:

the delivered hardware and/or required for system operation and support, categorized as
prohibited, restricted, or tracked, will be mutually agreed upon by the Government and
contractor.

blocks.text:
a. Prohibited HAZMAT require the contractor to obtain Government approval before 
those materials can be included in the system, subsystems, and support equipment or planned for 
system operation or support.

those materials can be included in the system, subsystems, and support equipment or planned for
system operation or support.

blocks.text:

or minimization.

or minimization.

blocks.text:
c. Tracked HAZMAT are those materials that do not require specific contractor action 
other than tracking and reporting.

other than tracking and reporting.

blocks.text:

when mutually agreed upon by both the Government and contractor.

when mutually agreed upon by both the Government and contractor.

TASK 201 
PRELIMINARY HAZARD LIST
text_blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 201
PRELIMINARY HAZARD LIST

text_blocks.text: 201.1 Purpose. Task 201 is to compile a list of potential hazards early in development.
text_blocks.normalized_text: 201.1 Purpose. Task 201 is to compile a list of potential hazards early in development.

text_blocks.text: 201.2 Task description. The contractor shall:
text_blocks.normalized_text: 201.2 Task description. The contractor shall:

text_blocks.text:
201.2.1 Examine the system shortly after the materiel solution analysis begins and 
compile a Preliminary Hazard List (PHL) identifying potential hazards inherent in the concept.
text_blocks.normalized_text:
201.2.1 Examine the system shortly after the materiel solution analysis begins and
compile a Preliminary Hazard List (PHL) identifying potential hazards inherent in the concept.

text_blocks.text:
201.2.2 Review historical documentation on similar and legacy systems, including but 
not limited to:
text_blocks.normalized_text:
201.2.2 Review historical documentation on similar and legacy systems, including but
not limited to:

text_blocks.text: a. Mishap and incident reports.
text_blocks.normalized_text: a. Mishap and incident reports.

text_blocks.text: b. Hazard tracking systems.
text_blocks.normalized_text: b. Hazard tracking systems.

text_blocks.text: c. Lessons learned.
text_blocks.normalized_text: c. Lessons learned.

text_blocks.text: d. Safety analyses and assessments.
text_blocks.normalized_text: d. Safety analyses and assessments.

text_blocks.text: e. Health hazard information.
text_blocks.normalized_text: e. Health hazard information.

text_blocks.text: f. Test documentation.
text_blocks.normalized_text: f. Test documentation.

text_blocks.text:
g. Environmental issues at potential locations for system testing, training, 
fielding/basing, and maintenance (organizational and depot).
text_blocks.normalized_text:
g. Environmental issues at potential locations for system testing, training,
fielding/basing, and maintenance (organizational and depot).

text_blocks.text:
h. Documentation associated with National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) and 
Executive Order (EO) 12114, Environmental Effects Abroad of Major Federal Actions.
text_blocks.normalized_text:
h. Documentation associated with National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) and
Executive Order (EO) 12114, Environmental Effects Abroad of Major Federal Actions.

text_blocks.text: i. Demilitarization and disposal plans.
text_blocks.normalized_text: i. Demilitarization and disposal plans.

text_blocks.text:
201.2.3 The contractor shall document identified hazards in the Hazard Tracking System 
(HTS). Contents and formats will be as agreed upon between the contractor and the Program 
Office. Unless otherwise specified in 201.3.d, minimum content shall included:
text_blocks.normalized_text:
201.2.3 The contractor shall document identified hazards in the Hazard Tracking System
(HTS). Contents and formats will be as agreed upon between the contractor and the Program
Office. Unless otherwise specified in 201.3.d, minimum content shall included:

text_blocks.text: a. A brief description of the hazard.
text_blocks.normalized_text: a. A brief description of the hazard.

text_blocks.text: b. The causal factor(s) for each identified hazard.
text_blocks.normalized_text: b. The causal factor(s) for each identified hazard.

text_blocks.text:
201.3 Details to be specified. The Request for Proposal (RFP) and Statement of Work (SOW) 
shall include the following, as applicable:
text_blocks.normalized_text:
201.3 Details to be specified. The Request for Proposal (RFP) and Statement of Work (SOW)
shall include the following, as applicable:

text_blocks.text: a. Imposition of Task 201. (R)
text_blocks.normalized_text: a. Imposition of Task 201. (R)

blocks.text:
TASK 202 
PRELIMINARY HAZARD ANALYSIS
blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 202

blocks.text:
TASK 202 
PRELIMINARY HAZARD ANALYSIS
blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 202
PRELIMINARY HAZARD ANALYSIS

blocks.text: TASK 202
blocks.normalized_text: TASK 202

blocks.text:
202.1 Purpose. Task 202 is to perform and document a Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) to 
identify hazards, assess the initial risks, and identify potential mitigation measures.
blocks.normalized_text:
202.1 Purpose. Task 202 is to perform and document a Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) to
identify hazards, assess the initial risks, and identify potential mitigation measures.

blocks.text:
202.2 Task description. The contractor shall perform and document a PHA to determine initial 
risk assessments of identified hazards. Hazards associated with the proposed design or function 
shall be evaluated for severity and probability based on the best available data, including mishap 
data (as accessible) from similar systems, legacy systems, and other lessons learned. Provisions, 
alternatives, and mitigation measures to eliminate hazards or reduce associated risk shall be 
included.
blocks.normalized_text:
202.2 Task description. The contractor shall perform and document a PHA to determine initial
risk assessments of identified hazards. Hazards associated with the proposed design or function
shall be evaluated for severity and probability based on the best available data, including mishap
data (as accessible) from similar systems, legacy systems, and other lessons learned. Provisions,
alternatives, and mitigation measures to eliminate hazards or reduce associated risk shall be
included.

blocks.text:
202.2.1 The contractor shall document the results of the PHA in the Hazard Tracking 
System (HTS).
blocks.normalized_text:
202.2.1 The contractor shall document the results of the PHA in the Hazard Tracking
System (HTS).

blocks.text: 202.2.1 The contractor shall document the results of the PHA in the Hazard Tracking
blocks.normalized_text: 202.2.1 The contractor shall document the results of the PHA in the Hazard Tracking

blocks.text:
202.2.2 The PHA shall identify hazards by considering the potential contribution to 
subsystem or system mishaps from:
blocks.normalized_text:
202.2.2 The PHA shall identify hazards by considering the potential contribution to
subsystem or system mishaps from:

blocks.text: 202.2.2 The PHA shall identify hazards by considering the potential contribution to
blocks.normalized_text: 202.2.2 The PHA shall identify hazards by considering the potential contribution to

blocks.text: a. System components.
blocks.normalized_text: a. System components.

blocks.text: b. Energy sources.
blocks.normalized_text: b. Energy sources.

blocks.text: c. Ordnance.
blocks.normalized_text: c. Ordnance.

blocks.text: d. Hazardous Materials (HAZMAT).
blocks.normalized_text: d. Hazardous Materials (HAZMAT).

blocks.text: e. Interfaces and controls.
blocks.normalized_text: e. Interfaces and controls.

blocks.text:
f. Interface considerations to other systems when in a network or System-of-Systems 
(SoS) architecture.
blocks.normalized_text:
f. Interface considerations to other systems when in a network or System-of-Systems
(SoS) architecture.

blocks.text: g. Material compatibilities.
blocks.normalized_text: g. Material compatibilities.

blocks.text: h. Inadvertent activation.
blocks.normalized_text: h. Inadvertent activation.

blocks.text:
i. Commercial-Off-the-Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Non- 
Developmental Items (NDIs), and Government-Furnished Equipment (GFE).
blocks.normalized_text:
i. Commercial-Off-the-Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Non-
Developmental Items (NDIs), and Government-Furnished Equipment (GFE).

blocks.text:
j. Software, including software developed by other contractors or sources. Design 
criteria to control safety-significant software commands and responses (e.g., inadvertent 
command, failure to command, untimely command or responses, and inappropriate magnitude) 
shall be identified, and appropriate action shall be taken to incorporate these into the software 
(and related hardware) specifications.
blocks.normalized_text:
j. Software, including software developed by other contractors or sources. Design
criteria to control safety-significant software commands and responses (e.g., inadvertent
command, failure to command, untimely command or responses, and inappropriate magnitude)
shall be identified, and appropriate action shall be taken to incorporate these into the software
(and related hardware) specifications.

blocks.text: k. Operating environment and constraints.
blocks.normalized_text: k. Operating environment and constraints.

blocks.text: TASK 203

blocks.text:
203.1 Purpose. Task 203 is to perform and document a System Requirements Hazard Analysis 
(SRHA) to determine the design requirements to eliminate hazards or reduce the associated risks 
for a system, to incorporate these requirements into the appropriate system documentation, and 
to assess compliance of the system with these requirements. The SRHA addresses all life-cycle 
phases and modes.

blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 204
SUBSYSTEM HAZARD ANALYSIS

blocks.text: TASK 204
blocks.normalized_text: TASK 204

blocks.text:
204.1 Purpose. Task 204 is to perform and document a Subsystem Hazard Analysis (SSHA) to 
verify subsystem compliance with requirements to eliminate hazards or reduce the associated 
risks; to identify previously unidentified hazards associated with the design of subsystems; and, 
to recommend actions necessary to eliminate identified hazards or mitigate their associated risks.
blocks.normalized_text:
204.1 Purpose. Task 204 is to perform and document a Subsystem Hazard Analysis (SSHA) to
verify subsystem compliance with requirements to eliminate hazards or reduce the associated
risks; to identify previously unidentified hazards associated with the design of subsystems; and,
to recommend actions necessary to eliminate identified hazards or mitigate their associated risks.

blocks.text:
204.2 Task description. The contractor shall perform and document an SSHA to identify 
hazards and mitigation measures in components and equipment. This analysis shall include 
Commercial-Off-the-Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Government-Furnished 
Equipment (GFE), Non-Developmental Items (NDI), and software. Areas to consider include 
performance, performance degradation, functional failures, timing errors, design errors or 
defects, and inadvertent functioning. While conducting this analysis, the human shall be 
considered a component within a subsystem, receiving both inputs and initiating outputs.
blocks.normalized_text:
204.2 Task description. The contractor shall perform and document an SSHA to identify
hazards and mitigation measures in components and equipment. This analysis shall include
Commercial-Off-the-Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Government-Furnished
Equipment (GFE), Non-Developmental Items (NDI), and software. Areas to consider include
performance, performance degradation, functional failures, timing errors, design errors or
defects, and inadvertent functioning. While conducting this analysis, the human shall be
considered a component within a subsystem, receiving both inputs and initiating outputs.

blocks.text: 204.2.1 At a minimum, the analysis shall:
blocks.normalized_text: 204.2.1 At a minimum, the analysis shall:

blocks.text: 204.2.1 At a minimum, the analysis shall:
blocks.normalized_text: 204.2.1 At a minimum, the analysis shall:

blocks.text:
a. Verify subsystem compliance with requirements to eliminate hazards or reduce the 
associated risks.
blocks.normalized_text:
a. Verify subsystem compliance with requirements to eliminate hazards or reduce the
associated risks.

blocks.text:
(1) Validate applicable flow-down of design requirements from top-level specifications 
to detailed design specifications for the subsystem.
blocks.normalized_text:
(1) Validate applicable flow-down of design requirements from top-level specifications
to detailed design specifications for the subsystem.

blocks.text:
(2) Ensure design criteria in the subsystem specifications have been satisfied and that 
verification and validation of subsystem mitigation measures have been included in test plans 
and procedures.
blocks.normalized_text:
(2) Ensure design criteria in the subsystem specifications have been satisfied and that
verification and validation of subsystem mitigation measures have been included in test plans
and procedures.

blocks.text: b. Identify previously unidentified hazards associated with the design of subsystems.
blocks.normalized_text: b. Identify previously unidentified hazards associated with the design of subsystems.

blocks.text:
(1) Ensure implementation of subsystem design requirements and mitigation measures 
have not introduced any new hazards.
blocks.normalized_text:
(1) Ensure implementation of subsystem design requirements and mitigation measures
have not introduced any new hazards.

blocks.text:
(2) Determine modes of failure, including component failure modes and human errors, 
single point and common mode failures, the effects when failures occur in subsystem 
components, and from functional relationships between components and equipment comprising 
each subsystem. Consider the potential contribution of subsystem hardware and software events 
(including those developed by other contractors/sources, COTS, GOTS, NDIs, and GFE 
hardware or software), faults, and occurrences (such as improper timing).
blocks.normalized_text:
(2) Determine modes of failure, including component failure modes and human errors,
single point and common mode failures, the effects when failures occur in subsystem
components, and from functional relationships between components and equipment comprising
each subsystem. Consider the potential contribution of subsystem hardware and software events
(including those developed by other contractors/sources, COTS, GOTS, NDIs, and GFE
hardware or software), faults, and occurrences (such as improper timing).

blocks.text:
c. Recommend actions necessary to eliminate previously unidentified hazards or mitigate 
their associated risk. Ensure system-level hazards attributed to the subsystem are analyzed and 
adequate mitigations of the potential hazards are implemented in the design.
blocks.normalized_text:
c. Recommend actions necessary to eliminate previously unidentified hazards or mitigate
their associated risk. Ensure system-level hazards attributed to the subsystem are analyzed and
adequate mitigations of the potential hazards are implemented in the design.

direction be provided to the software developers.

TASK 205 
SYSTEM HAZARD ANALYSIS
blocks.normalized_text:
TASK 205

TASK 205

TASK 205
SYSTEM HAZARD ANALYSIS

blocks.text: TASK 205
blocks.normalized_text: TASK 205

blocks.text:
205.2 Task description. The contractor shall perform and document an SHA to identify hazards 
and mitigation measures in the integrated system design, including software and subsystem and 
human interfaces. This analysis shall include interfaces associated with Commercial-Off-the- 
Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Government-Furnished Equipment (GFE), 
Non-Developmental Items (NDI), and software. Areas to consider include performance, 
performance degradation, functional failures, timing errors, design errors or defects, and 
inadvertent functioning. While conducting this analysis, the human shall be considered a 
component within the system, receiving both inputs and initiating outputs.
blocks.normalized_text:
205.2 Task description. The contractor shall perform and document an SHA to identify hazards
and mitigation measures in the integrated system design, including software and subsystem and
human interfaces. This analysis shall include interfaces associated with Commercial-Off-the-
Shelf (COTS), Government-Off-the-Shelf (GOTS), Government-Furnished Equipment (GFE),
Non-Developmental Items (NDI), and software. Areas to consider include performance,
performance degradation, functional failures, timing errors, design errors or defects, and
inadvertent functioning. While conducting this analysis, the human shall be considered a
component within the system, receiving both inputs and initiating outputs.

blocks.text: 205.2.1 This analysis shall include a review of subsystems interrelationships for:
blocks.normalized_text: 205.2.1 This analysis shall include a review of subsystems interrelationships for:

blocks.normalized_text: 205.2.1 This analysis shall include a review of subsystems interrelationships for:
